item.title: Langford's Murder Attempts
item.content:encoded: As someone who has been through the traumatic event that is a loved one committing suicide, I'm baffled by Langford's decision to try to assist the SCP in killing herself. I suppose she could have thought of it as an act of mercy, but that isn't necessarily made clear in the article. It would've made more sense (to me, at least) if she had desperately redoubled her efforts to get the SCP to a better state of mind, especially considering her profession as a psychiatrist. This… just sort of comes off as a startling out-of-the-blue decision to straight-up kill someone who needs her help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After reading all the comments, I quite like what the author tried to achieve here. But though I don't agree with all the issues Mendelssohn found here, it still ends up seeming too much of a Sympathetic Sue.

I won't repeat arguments if I can avoid it, though. Instead, I'll try to throw some suggestions.

+ Trim down the Containment Procedures
- They don't need so many details

I can understand your concern with avoiding criticism of people looking for holes in your SCP, but by indulging them so much you fall into a trap. This is first and foremost a writing exercise, and you can use some simple techniques to avoid detailing technical stuff that won't add to your story.

Most of your procedures can be easily replaced by standardization. She needs a standard humanoid cell, a standard nutrition for her size and age, and standard amenities for cooperative medium-danger entities. Even her suicidal tendencies can be contained by level 3 suicide watch.

Then, you can focus on what really is important for her containment: she can't be touched or seen in the eyes, and her suicide watch must be adapted for such conditions. This is also a good place to add with brevity some plushy toys and other amenities that resemble human contact, just don't overdo it. The access to the forum of her victims is a neat add, just need some trimming, and maybe that computer could have some games to play with others either through external or internal connection.

- They don't need so many details
+ Revise your dates
- I'm surprised no one noticed

She was born 1996. Her anomalous abilities became apparent seemingly at age 16. That is, around 2012. She was supposedly contained a couple years later, at least, and remained in confinement for almost a decade. But all the dates, though blanked, are before 2010.

I recommend that you focus on establishing her age at each point of the story, and drop the dates altogether. If you want to keep them, you'll need far more attention to numbers, but then you don't need to blank them.

As a pet peeve of mine, I suggest using the months names instead of numbers. There is no international standard about using first day or month, and it causes confusion to me.

- I'm surprised no one noticed
+ Simplify her psychiatric profile on Description
- Details should be elsewhere

It is a matter of organizing information. Her profile is an important thing, for sure, but that is not the place for too much details on technical information. Not only it makes her seem like a Sue, if this was a real document it would just make the main presentation of her clumsy, while not actually helping the team that really needs this info.

Also, as a matter of fact, you can simply say she have severe depression, evolving to mild during containment, with suicide attempts during less severe periods. She didn't show any other psychiatric problem, severe or otherwise, and suicide during depression actually comes during the good days. Your depiction of this during the log was accurate enough.

- Details should be elsewhere
+ Keep the Description more clinical
- It's not hard

The description is quite good by now, but it still have a bunch of personal touches that could be reserved for the logs and addenda.

Cut out the reference to family and friends on the second paragraph, 58% of a town's population is info enough, and already implies people close to her. The third paragraph needs more trimming.

You can try to exchange the entirety of third paragraph for more specific info: date and conditions of Foundation awareness, predicted age of onset of the anomaly, the fact that she was already aware of it when caught, and that evidence points to her having no control over the anomaly. You can put here the theoretical link between closeness and onset of tumours, but everything else is redundant or should be elsewhere. Also, since contact is likely to be more frequent between close acquaintances, you can even drop this link completely, that would be even better.

- It's not hard
+ Take down the amnestic treatment
- It isn't helpful

The medication log is actually a good idea. It helps to show that she have a condition and that it is relevant, that she is a person, all the while keeping it cold without being ruthless.

You can even expand on this, using changes in medication, like adding food supplements, to show her evolution in confinement. Reducing medication on certain dates can tell as much as adding it.

But she is the main source of information about her anomaly, and even if they work, amnestics won't help the purpose of her containment. Adding it seems out of purpose just for the sake of making her miserable, and raises tangential questions on why she is immune, questions that only would get in the way, here.

- It isn't helpful
+ Fuse logs 1 and 2
- They're ultimately redundant

Those two logs are good on their own right. They start to establish the characters of Lily and Langford, and still serve the IC purpose of informing how the skip relates to the containment personnel. But they could be conflated into one, since they mostly serve the same purpose.

On the notes, I would change the one about Langford being reprimanded for some of her observations on Lily's drawings. You see, Langford IS her therapist, and Lily is depressed. It is expected that Langford herself had prescribed the use of personal names to help on the improvement of the mental state of the skip. To force the only person she talks to to use a number instead of her name would be cruel, not cold.

All the while, the drawings can both better describe her character to the reader and be research materials for the Foundation: they can be analyzed for anomalous effect, they can be used to look for subconscious info on her anomaly, and they can be used to pry information from her in the future, be it by threats, negotiations or simply encouragement.

- They're ultimately redundant
+ Rewrite the third log
- Right now it is just a festival of tears

Now, the third paragraph is a key point of the skip. First, it is where she talks about how it began. Second, it is where Langford start to worry too much. Third, it is the first time she crumbles in front of the reader.

By no means it should be just removed, everything before was just preparation for this one log, but precisely because of that it must be written with extra care.

Some things to keep in mind:

1- Langford is supposed to ask those questions now, and it was avoided for too much time. And that is no secret. Even her therapy needs this moment, therapy isn't about doing idle talk all the time, it is about talking the hard talk.

2- Though it isn't yet the last draw, this is the point where Langford should undoubtedly show that she cares more than would be healthy for her context. A name isn't enough, and Lily being miserable isn't the point. Langford needs her own moment here to do something just lightly inappropriate, but certainly dooming on the long run.

3- What is really hard is to drive the breakdown without making it too melodramatic. You can start it making the characters clash more. Lily really don't want to talk it, but she have too. Neither she should stop easily to avoid it, nor Langford should let her get away from topic. There is little, though, that can be said before a rewrite of the log.

- Right now it is just a festival of tears
+ Take down the last log as it is now
- make it anew or put a descriptive addendum in its place

At one side, the purpose of this log is clear. It is the point where Langford's crosses the line and tries to give some rest to Lily, through unprofessional means.

But as it is now, it is extremely redundant. It just hammers down how miserable Lily is, beyond hope, and adds nothing to the previous ones except Langford's final decision.

You can replace it by a simplest entry just saying that Langford interviewed Lily without recording after a suicide attempt, and let it be.

Alternatively, you should actually add something new to whatever entry you put here. It could be an Incident log of her latest attempt at suicide, Langford being the one to interfere, and the exchange occur during the event. Or Lily could actually add something new about what happened in the past, or any new information, anomalous or personal, that explains a greater reason for her to feel hopeless (she doesn't need to be right, just to feel hopeless). Langford could even been interviewing some affected victim instead of Lily, this time.

- make it anew or put a descriptive addendum in its place
+ Cut down the O5
- That is quite direct

This is a minor nitpick here, but there is no need for an O5 to be called on this. Any on-site superior can make the call, and there is no need to specify who manages transfers between sites. The O5 are the highest management positions in the entire Foundation, be it a massive or minimalist Foundation, and very unlikely to intervene in this kind of situation. It is like having the company's CEO intervene.

Either way, on a general note this part seems mostly ok. Langford is the main focus on this, and this is her downfall, simple and believable. If you trim enough of the rest of the article, you can even expand here on a log of Langford attempting to reach and kill Lily. Or you can work on how things evolved under Rosetta. Not something big, either way.

- That is quite direct

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Response
- Close

I find myself agreeing with you on a lot of points while feeling like holding up the 'your mileage may vary' card in terms of interpretation. Cop-out, I know, but bear with me.

This is redundant IMO. Most humanoids should be allowed to do this. The footnote here is also rather unnecessary. The Foundation would already know that some stuff is toxic and should not be given in large amounts.

The Foundation, sure, but it only takes one person slipping up to do serious damage. The risk, however small, warrants the footnote for me.

Probably redundant, because I believe it should be common knowledge that someone should be restrained when absolutely necessary.

The point of this sentence isn't that she should be restrained when necessary, it's that personnel are instructed to avoid physical restraints whenever possible.

This undernourishment thing seems random and disjointed, like it's been put in here to pad the article out and nothing else. It's just brought up, and then, aside from one random line in the description, totally neglected.

Zachary explained to me what would happen if someone would attempt to starve themselves in an attempt to end their life. We're both social workers, but he has more experience with these precautions than I do.

The main reason for so much detail about the precautions is mainly because I didn't want people to point out obvious ways for her to commit suicide that I missed. Instead, it created a situation for you where the detail seem unnecessary. That's fairly hard to balance.

This is redundant. The average humanoid containment unit will always come with standard sanitation and minimal furniture requirements.

Yes, which is why it's only part of the sentence.

Probably also redundant. Most humanoids should be able to have access to accomodations to improve their mental health. Unless what they need is specific to them (I redirect you to SCP-3872 again), it kind of feels unnecessary to state these things.

Yeah, I'll give you that one. I'll think about removing that entirely.

Physical contact with what? Physical contact with the walls of the containment chamber? Physical contact with SCP-3550's personal effects? Physical contact with SCP-682?

Added: Physical contact with SCP-3550 is strictly prohibited without use of protective gloves.

Why do we need to know this? Anyone can pull out the hair on their scalp. This has nothing to do with its anomalous properties, and is going into excessive and irrelevant detail about your humanoid's appearance. That is frowned upon.

It's just a sentence; I'm not dedicating a paragraph describing the specific shade of her eye colour.

Anomaly should be the first priority, not physical and mental state. Unless the anomaly is directly connected to their mental state, focusing on the humanoid's mental state over their anomalous properties in an article, to me, would be a severe thematic mismatch.

Yeah, I guess that's true. I'll probably switch those around.

This should go in Containment Procedures.

Right.

You know, if she really desires physical comfort that badly, it wouldn't hurt to give her extra pillows or stuffed toys to hug. Comfort objects such as soft blankets and plushies can certainly do a lot improve one's psychological wellbeing.

That's a good idea, actually. Thanks!

Formatting broken

Ah, blast.

Conceptually, this still reads like a Sympathetic Sue. She was a normal person but then on one day, for no reason at all, spontaneously caused everyone around her to contract terrible diseases and die. And people she likes will die faster.

I take issue with this, since 3550 doesn't have the unnatural magnetism that the archetype usually has. Langford is the only one that breaks her professional role to comfort her, which is the reason for her eventual removal of 3550's care. What I WILL agree on, is that I still haven't found a reasonable way for her anomaly to have manifested without making the cause a cartoon-villain or a ridiculously contrived accident akin to a gypsy curse.

The anomaly still gives me the overwhelming impression that it was only created to give her a reason to be as miserable and depressed as possible.

No, to be honest I wanted to create conflict inside the Foundation itself. I wanted to show humanity in people that work at a place where sympathy and attachment is dangerous and reckless.

If I am to be harsher, I would say SCP-3550 here is essentially a cursed human with no defined context or backstory, and the reader is pretty much forced to accept that she exists, leaving all their questions about her and the nature of her anomalous properties totally neglected.

Again, you're right on some level. I have difficulty finding a plausible cause, but I did write the article with the above goal in mind, not just making her miserable.

The anomaly here is only utilized for telling a story that is tangentially related to the anomaly itself at best, and that makes it feel like the anomaly is merely an afterthought. That's a thematic mismatch for an SCP article, which is supposed to resemble a scientific report on an anomaly meant for fellow scientists to read.

If I were to go into more detail about the anomaly itself, would that really solve your issue with it? Last time you left feedback you told me that it's only showing the worse moments, no room for character development. Now that I completely remade the interview showing happier moments and interaction that doesn't serve as a source of more misery, you instead say that the it's a thematic mismatch to do so. So what do you propose I do instead?

This really makes it feel like the anomaly was shoved in at the last minute.

It wasn't, I came up with it chat once, I believe the conversation was about a raid on anomalous humanoids that didn't want to hurt anyone.

I also picked up on a great deal of thematic confusion in the article. I cannot tell if you aim to present a more sympathetic Foundation or a colder one. We have a Foundation that really focuses on the "Protect" aspect of the Foundation mantra, so much as to the point all suicide/self-harm prevention measures and medications and therapy sessions full of idle chitchat are meticulously detailed, but at the same time, the Foundation is unsympathetic and doesn't care about her? It confuses me.

That was the point, yes. The Foundation isn't one entity, they're multiple people that disagree on what doing the right thing is.

Also, you made a statement earlier that you wanted to write about an SCP as a person without the Foundation treating it as one. If that is true, then I do not believe you succeeded. This SCP is definitely treated like a person - suicide prevention measures are illustrated in detail, the article treats her mental health as especially important

Ah, but is that because she's being sympathised with or because the Foundation feels that she should be alive and the detail serves that purpose?

It implies that between suicide and rehabilitation, the preferable option is the former, and doing everything one possibly can to stop someone from killing themselves in the name of protecting them is cruel and heartless. I believe that that is a very unhealthy message to send across.

That's not what I mean to say and I think you know that as well. I appreciate that you take the time to do this but I ask of you not to be hyperbolic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, crit time:

+ takin' another look
- I'm persnickety.

Special Containment Procedures:

SCP-3550 has refused to eat several times since initial containment.

We probably do not need this in the Containment Procedures, because the Procedures are supposed to be instructions telling personnel how to contain and maintain an anomalous entity.

To this end, SCP-3550 is allowed to request off-menu nutritional items to a limited degree

This is redundant IMO. Most humanoids should be allowed to do this. The footnote here is also rather unnecessary. The Foundation would already know that some stuff is toxic and should not be given in large amounts.

SCP-3550 is to be restrained only when absolutely necessary.

Probably redundant, because I believe it should be common knowledge that someone should be restrained when absolutely necessary.

When undernourishment is judged to be present, Fortisip or intravenous food drip can be administered at the discretion of both Dr. Rosetta and the Ethics Committee.

This undernourishment thing seems random and disjointed, like it's been put in here to pad the article out and nothing else. It's just brought up, and then, aside from one random line in the description, totally neglected. I would suggest either removing this undernourishment point completely or bringing it up in the interview logs. Too much fluff information can really hurt an article.

Along with standard sanitation and minimal furniture requirements

This is redundant. The average humanoid containment unit will always come with standard sanitation and minimal furniture requirements.

Accommodations to improve SCP-3550's mental health have been requested and approved, such as reading material, radio and television.

Probably also redundant. Most humanoids should be able to have access to accomodations to improve their mental health. Unless what they need is specific to them (I redirect you to SCP-3872 again), it kind of feels unnecessary to state these things.

Dr. Rosetta has evaluated that SCP-3550 has a medium to high risk of suicide. Precautions have been taken to minimize risk of self-harm. The containment chamber contains no furniture higher than SCP-3550's waist, and no sharp objects and edges are allowed inside the containment cell. SCP-3550's garments are devoid of items that may assist in self-termination such as shoelaces, belts and ties.

1. First sentence probably is not necessary; stating that there are suicide prevention measures already implies that the entity is at risk of suicide.
2. Might just be a me thing, but the overall vibe of this paragraph gives me the impression that keeping Seward here from committing suicide is the most important thing and is the highest priority. That I feel is rather unfair, since a significant majority of humanoid anomalies are psychologically unstable in some way or another, and there is no discernible reason why keeping this one person from suicide is more important than preventing the suicide of all the other humanoids that are potentially at risk of suicide. My judgment may be wrong though, so take this comment with a grain of salt.

Physical contact is strictly prohibited without use of protective gloves.

Physical contact with what? Physical contact with the walls of the containment chamber? Physical contact with SCP-3550's personal effects? Physical contact with SCP-682?

Overall:
I find that it is weighted down by too much redundant and unnecessary information, which is somewhat off-putting. My opinion that this section could use thorough pruning and tidying still stands.

Description:

There are several places on its scalp where the hair has been forcefully removed

Why do we need to know this? Anyone can pull out the hair on their scalp. This has nothing to do with its anomalous properties, and is going into excessive and irrelevant detail about your humanoid's appearance. That is frowned upon.

SCP-3550 is a Caucasian female, born in 1996. SCP-3550 is physically healthy with the exception of mild malnutrition. There are several places on its scalp where the hair has been forcefully removed. It has a history of severe psychological problems since its initial recovery. █ suicide attempts have been recorded since containment began in 200█ (see extended psychiatric evaluation).

Anomaly should be the first priority, not physical and mental state. Unless the anomaly is directly connected to their mental state, focusing on the humanoid's mental state over their anomalous properties in an article, to me, would be a severe thematic mismatch.

SCP-3550 receives weekly therapy sessions on Wednesday at 18:00 performed by Dr. Rosetta in treatment of clinical depression and general anxiety disorder. SCP-3550 is required to take 20mg 40mg of fluoxetine daily as prescribed by Dr. Langford.

This should go in Containment Procedures.

Dialogue stuff:

I'll never be able to see or feel anyone for the rest of my life. Would you want to live like that?

You know, if she really desires physical comfort that badly, it wouldn't hurt to give her extra pillows or stuffed toys to hug. Comfort objects such as soft blankets and plushies can certainly do a lot improve one's psychological wellbeing.

** (Irrelevant dialogue removed)**

Formatting broken

Concept, content, theme, narrative, etc:

Conceptually, this still reads like a Sympathetic Sue. She was a normal person but then on one day, for no reason at all, spontaneously caused everyone around her to contract terrible diseases and die. And people she likes will die faster. And she can never forget that they died. Think about that for a moment. The anomaly still gives me the overwhelming impression that it was only created to give her a reason to be as miserable and depressed as possible. It also gives the impression that this character was designed as a device meant to squeeze as much sympathy out of the reader as possible, and not as a realistic character.

If I am to be harsher, I would say SCP-3550 here is essentially a cursed human with no defined context or backstory, and the reader is pretty much forced to accept that she exists, leaving all their questions about her and the nature of her anomalous properties totally neglected.

In fact, you could replace the anomaly with a lot of other things - say, she accidentally left the stove on and that resulted in a huge fire that burned half her town to ashes - and the plot would stay pretty much the same. The anomaly here is only utilized for telling a story that is tangentially related to the anomaly itself at best, and that makes it feel like the anomaly is merely an afterthought. That's a thematic mismatch for an SCP article, which is supposed to resemble a scientific report on an anomaly meant for fellow scientists to read.

On the topic of thematic mismatch, the article essentially gives us an outline of an object with some anomalous properties, and then goes off repeatedly bombarding the reader with "this human has severe psychological problems!!!" This really makes it feel like the anomaly was shoved in at the last minute. The bulk of the article revolves around chanting that she has psychological health issues, as if she were the only one around suffering from those. Sure, taking note of a sapient anomaly's mental health is important, but when the Foundation deals with people afflicted with all sorts of mental health issues daily, it would make no sense to continually harp on how one of them is depressed or something. In addition, the main focal point of each article ought to be the anomaly, not the mental illness.

If I am to be harsh again, I'd say that the way SCP-3550 is presented overall stikes me as being more reminiscent of a Sympathetic Sue than a humanoid SCP.

I also picked up on a great deal of thematic confusion in the article. I cannot tell if you aim to present a more sympathetic Foundation or a colder one. We have a Foundation that really focuses on the "Protect" aspect of the Foundation mantra, so much as to the point all suicide/self-harm prevention measures and medications and therapy sessions full of idle chitchat are meticulously detailed, but at the same time, the Foundation is unsympathetic and doesn't care about her? It confuses me.

Also, you made a statement earlier that you wanted to write about an SCP as a person without the Foundation treating it as one. If that is true, then I do not believe you succeeded. This SCP is definitely treated like a person - suicide prevention measures are illustrated in detail, the article treats her mental health as especially important, her medications are extensively described, her idle conversations are preserved, etc. The Foundation does not appear sufficiently detached and distant to give the reader the impression that they aren't treating this humanoid like a person.

Now onto another problematic aspect of the article which I had previously not addressed: the underlying message.

Thinking about what the above commenters said was tragic - that this person wishes for death but is prevented from doing so, that the Foundation is cruel and unfeeling for stopping her - that message is frankly in bad taste and very hostile. It implies that between suicide and rehabilitation, the preferable option is the former, and doing everything one possibly can to stop someone from killing themselves in the name of protecting them is cruel and heartless. I believe that that is a very unhealthy message to send across.

EDIT: SCP-3550 also seems like a more weakly presented (IMO) version of the reality bender in this tale. Don't know if this is a coincidence or not.

Feel free to refute any of these points, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now this is constructive! I'll thoroughly consider and implement your point wherever I can, thanks for that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this was a little heavy-handed with the theme - the Foundation are completely unfeeling jerks (I guess? because they won't let this poor girl kill herself?) and Dr Langford is presented as a tragic hero by comparison. That seems overblown and unrealistic to me. My feelings seem out of step with the comments above, however.

If it were up to me, I'd say there were two options to improve the story of the article:

1) Reduce the blatancy of the emotional manipulation, and build 3550's character. Easiest way to do that is to completely replace the interviews - this article currently falls into a common trap of making the interviews directly expository, rather than indirectly. Why not make them some of the later therapy sessions, so you can show an already-developed relationship between the doctor and the skip. Read up (or if relevant consider any experience you have) on how therapy works, and perhaps make it a conversation about something other than her condition (which can't be good therapy to talk about it all the time!). Then show how grief and guilt can intrude on that conversation - I think that will make for a more natural illustration of both the skip's personality and her plight.

If you take this line, I'd consider removing some of the most blatant "cold" Foundation stuff. The "I'm not a number" and "Its" stuff is heavy-handed.

2) Subvert expectations. People expect the "Foundation is cold" narrative, but what if you show that Dr Langford is wrong. What if the skip makes progress with Dr Rosetta, and is no longer suicidal? What does that say about the Foundation's decisions, and Dr Langford's?

Or what if (after the amnestics) Dr Langford is put back to treating the skip, and then she starts making progress? You could have three interviews (the third one with a subtly more distant Dr Langford and a psychologically improved skip), and then spring the amnestics surprise in the logs.

Either way, there are also improvements to be made in the drafting. The Containment Procedures, although long, are pretty good and engaging, but I found the Description slow. I would move your fourth paragraph up to become the second paragraph, I would thoroughly rewrite that paragraph for clarity (is it me making eye contact with 3550 that causes me cancer? If so, why do the goggles help? If not, what does the stuff about video feed and photographs mean?). Finally, I would delete the first collapsible and the following material, which doesn't really add anything, and skip straight to the interviews.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Everyone have their own opinions, don't worry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm also a depression sufferer but this didn't turn out emotional in any way for me. Is that bad?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy crap, as someone who suffered from depression before, this is emotional as hell.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. The article is not thematically in line with the Foundation. There is too much emphasis on the suffering and misery of the SCP, how she's suicidal and all that jazz, and very little emphasis on the anomalous properties. Unless her anomalous property is that she's constantly miserable and depressed.

''Protect'' is also a part of the Foundation mantra, that includes the SCP itself. Besides not being sure what happens if she does die, I tried to make sure that all information about her psychological state is adding to properly securing it better. Think about it: If you're containment personnel, you need to be aware of her suicidal tendencies because it would be too much of a risk if you're caught by surprise.

2. The SCP has next to no personality. Even with interview logs and stuff, there isn't much to her other than "constantly miserable", which IMO, is not realistic. Even when people are severely depressed, they still retain at least a few of their individual personality quirks.

I agree about the personality, but keep in mind that the format of an article like this only allows characterization from the perspective of the Foundation itself, AKA interview logs and the notes at the end. The Foundation considers the risk of suicide important, but her hobbies aren't something the security document on an SCP needs to have. Elaborating on personality outside of that would be weird in-universe.

3. My biggest gripe: the SCP is too melodramatic to be realistic. There's too much emphasis on her torment and too many terrible things happen to her without rhyme or reason. All in all it feels more like a clumsy attempt at "She is SUFFERING! Feel bad for her!" rather than a genuine tragedy. I can't feel sorry for her because the sheer misfortune she has gone through is just too over-the-top and it destroys my suspension of disbelief.

Well…you're certainly direct about that. That's fair.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still have a bunch of issues with the article. (my silly opinion)

1. The article is not thematically in line with the Foundation. There is too much emphasis on the suffering and misery of the SCP, how she's suicidal and all that jazz, and very little emphasis on the anomalous properties. Unless her anomalous property is that she's constantly miserable and depressed.

2. The SCP has next to no personality. Even with interview logs and stuff, there isn't much to her other than "constantly miserable", which IMO, is not realistic. Even when people are severely depressed, they still retain at least a few of their individual personality quirks.

3. My biggest gripe: the SCP is too melodramatic to be realistic. There's too much emphasis on her torment and too many terrible things happen to her without rhyme or reason. All in all it feels more like a clumsy attempt at "She is SUFFERING! Feel bad for her!" rather than a genuine tragedy. I can't feel sorry for her because the sheer misfortune she has gone through is just too over-the-top and it destroys my suspension of disbelief.

Down boat for now.

item.title: Re: It hit close to home
item.content:encoded: Just to clarify, in terms of malicious intent, I mean towards at her not her victims.

item.title: Re: It hit close to home
item.content:encoded:
I think what I wanted to prove to myself that you can write about an SCP as a person, without the Foundation treating an SCP like one.

I honestly wonder if the anomaly was created with malicious intent.

Who knows? If so, they should have chosen someone with less empathy.

She kills anyone who makes prolonged eye contact with her or her photos and video recordings,

That's the angle I went for, especially because it's not voluntary. She can't help it.

item.title: It hit close to home
item.content:encoded:
It was a tough read. it makes me wonder just how the foundation's higher ups can maintain such a cold demeanor.

I honestly wonder if the anomaly was created with malicious intent. She kills anyone who makes prolonged eye contact with her or her photos and video recordings, and amnestics only make her memory more vivid. These 2 anomalies don't seem to be connected in anyway but it could just be a coincidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much! It means a lot that you, among others, pick up so well on the themes I went for when writing this. Thanks for the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was damn fine. Langford's actions are more than a little unprofessional but IMO perfectly plausible considering 3550's situation. I love how this highlights the darker side of the 'cold not cruel' mantra that gets pushed around so often. It's an important facet of the Foundation's ethos which represents the crux of their moral ambiguity and I've never seen it exploited quite this well. I really look forward to more of your work and heartily +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Solid and tragic article! Great idea, writing, and execution. Definite +1. Saw it before as a draft and still shocked of how good it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you picked up on that! I tried to keep it subtle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's exactly what I was going for, thank you. You really understand what I tried to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Closing Statement: Dr. Langford: Her medication has been adjusted.

RE:Closing Statement: Site Overseer: ''Its'', Dr. Langford. ''Its'' medication has been adjusted.

I would change how this is stylized to match other SCPs, i.e:

Her medication has been adjusted — Dr. Langford

"Its", Dr. Langford, "Its" medication has been adjusted. — Site Overseer

Why do you keep me alive? Why do you risk your people? Haven't I done enough?

I personally feel like this reads awkwardly and would change it to something like "Why do you risk keeping me alive"

all said and done tho, +1 cool story

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like good tragedy (according to Aristotle) this evoked both fear and pity. The SCP has been 'abandoned' by the only sympathetic staff member, who in turn has been involuntarily made to forget.

Now we have a human being who longs only for death, which she is prevented from seeking. Grim, dark, but not grimdark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, good eye! I fixed it, thanks. And I'm glad to hear you like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A simple, well-written SCP. Good job.

One thing though: I think the formatting is a bit broken on the fourth entry in the extended psychiatric evaluation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love it, just like I did when it was a draft (: Sympathetic character, but not milking it.

item.title: Thanks and spoilers.
item.content:encoded:
Special thanks to Zachary Maxwell for the high-quality and in-depth feedback. SpookyPizza also noted me on a massive potential plothole that was thankfully corrected. avagadro_catalyst helped me get the medication right along with Zachary.

+ Spoilers for the notes 
- Close

05/06/200█: Termination request by Dr. Langford 05/06/200█: Denied. Object does not pose risk to personnel under current containment procedures. ~ Site Director.

This is where we start seeing the cracks on Dr. Langfords' psyche. She starts to pity SCP-3550 after extended therapy and lies to her Site Director about the risk of keeping SCP-3550 alive.

06/06/200█: Termination request by Dr. Langford 06/06/200█: Denied. ~ Dr. ███ - Senior Staff Ethics committee.

She then tries to convince the higher-ups. Same result.

07/06/200█: Prescription change to █████ by Dr. Langford: Denied due to risk of lethal overdose. ~ Senior Medical staff.

Langford is a psychiatrist, which means that this was a very dangerous mistake. Or was it a mistake? Langford probably did know that the drugs could be lethal. She tried to take matters into her own hands.

08/06/200█: Dr. Langford has been reassigned to another project by order of O5-█ and Site Overseer.

The Site Overseer is onto her now. They know that Langford is too close to SCP-3550 emotionally and decide to remove her.

09/06/200█: SCP-3550's mental condition has worsened severely. Attempts suicide around 0:23pm and has to be restrained by security personnel. No injuries. Security officer ████ requests reassignment. Request approved by Site Overseer.

It's possible that SCP-3550 was aware of Langford trying to end her life and tries to end her own again when she learns that Langford isn't coming back. The plan seemed to have failed.

09/06/200█: Dr. Langford requests to be returned to Site-42 for assignment at the earliest convenience to another object (SCP-████)on-site.

This is where Langford is starting to get desperate. She's getting obsessed with SCP-3550 and attempts to return to Site-42 under a poor excuse of wanting to work with another object close by.

10/06/200█: Dr. Langford has been administered Class-C Amnestics and will be reassigned off-site for the foreseeable future. She will be replaced by Dr. Rosetta effective immediately. ~ O5-█

This is where O5 decides she's over the line. It's decided that her memories of SCP-3550 are to be forcefully removed.

Finally, the name 'Cancer of Passion' is a play on words on the term 'Crime of Passion'.

Enjoy!

Edit: Thanks to Labiosis for pointing out inconsistencies with the dates!
